Were You Asked This?
Design a News Aggregator System

Design a news aggregation system like Google News that allows users to subscribe to different news sources, categories, and topics, then view a personalized real-time news feed based on their interests.

Asked at:

Rippling

R

ripping

Amazon

Microsoft

+6 More
Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

A news aggregator like Google News collects articles from many publishers, normalizes and deduplicates them, and then delivers a personalized, near real-time feed based on user subscriptions to sources, categories, and topics. Users follow interests (e.g., Bloomberg, "AI", Sports) and get a constantly updating stream of headlines, summaries, and links.

Interviewers ask this because it blends streaming ingestion, content normalization, feed serving, and real-time delivery. It tests whether you can design multi-stage pipelines, pick the right serving strategy (fanout-on-write vs. fanout-on-read), manage deduplication and idempotency, and hit latency targets with read-heavy workloads. The role-play often rewards candidates who scope ranking simply (rule-based) and focus on correctness, scale, and timely updates.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
News Aggregator

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to subscribe to news sources, categories, and keyword topics to personalize their feed.

Users should be able to view a personalized feed that updates in near real time with new and relevant stories.

Users should be able to manage preferences (mute/unfollow sources or topics, set language/region) and see those reflected quickly.

Users should be able to receive optional breaking-news notifications aligned with their interests.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you ingest and normalize content from thousands of heterogeneous sources reliably (RSS/Atom, publisher APIs, web pages) while respecting rate limits and ensuring idempotency?

How can you serve a personalized feed with low latency (<200 ms p95) at scale?

How will you deduplicate and cluster near-duplicate stories from different sources in near real time?

How will you deliver near real-time updates and notifications without overwhelming your system (fanout, connection scale, mobile push)?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

The product promise is a near real-time feed. You need a clear strategy for server-to-client update delivery (push vs. pull), connection management, and notification fanout that avoids synchronous broadcast storms while keeping perceived latency low.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Feeds are overwhelmingly read-heavy. Low-latency retrieval requires precomputation, caching, pagination, and indexes that let you avoid N-way joins at request time. This pattern is central to meeting p95 targets under high QPS.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

Ingestion is a pipeline: fetch → parse → normalize → dedupe/cluster → enrich → index → fanout. Treating it as a durable, observable workflow with retries and idempotency prevents data loss and keeps the system resilient.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka cleanly decouples fetchers from processors, absorbs spikes from publishers, and provides per-source partitioning for ordering and rate isolation. It enables scalable fanout to enrichment, dedupe, indexing, and notification services.

Read More
Redis

Redis offers in-memory sorted sets and lists ideal for hot feed pages, per-interest queues, and ephemeral session state. It also supports pub/sub patterns for lightweight real-time signals and can implement rate limiting for ingestion.

Read More
Elasticsearch

Elasticsearch provides fast full-text search, filters (facets), and relevance scoring across articles and clusters. It can also power "more like this" queries to aid dedupe/near-duplicate detection and topic-based discovery.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

News Aggregator

It is the same class of system: aggregate from many sources, dedupe/cluster, and deliver a personalized, continuously updating news feed with regional and topical personalization.

Read
Practice
FB News Feed

Both require personalization, feed precomputation vs. on-read trade-offs, caching, pagination, and real-time updates to active users without overwhelming the system.

Read
Practice
Web Crawler

Large-scale source ingestion, respectful rate limiting, parsing heterogeneous HTML/RSS formats, and robust retry/backoff behaviors closely mirror the aggregator’s fetch-and-normalize stage.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Skipping deduplication and idempotency in the ingestion pipeline

This leads to the same story appearing multiple times from syndicated sources, bloats storage, and corrupts analytics. Without stable keys and early dedupe, your feed quality and ranking logic are undermined.

Computing the personalized feed entirely on-demand without caching or precomputation

Joining fresh articles across all followed sources and topics per request will miss latency targets at scale and overload databases. You need precomputed candidate sets, caches, and incremental updates.

Synchronous fanout to every follower on each new article

Broadcasting to all subscribers in-line causes write amplification, hot partitions, and cascading failures during spikes. Use async pipelines, topic/interest queues, and push lightweight signals instead of full payloads.

User feeds should be generated based on categories they give to us (can assume that categories are already determined prior to generating feed)
Interviewer asked me deep dives on how to handle a lot of writes to database when many articles are being published at once. Interviewer asked me how I would handle database write scaling when one category in particular is hot. Interviewer asked me how to handle fault tolerance with article retrieval service (if write to DB fails, how do we ensure that we're not still uploading thumbnails to blob storage for unstored article).
